nodes.title: Sort a copy, not the original input

anchor.snippet: const arr = nums.slice().sort((a, b) => a - b);

nodes.why: Sorting enables two pointers, and `slice()` avoids mutating the caller input.
nodes.tradeoff: Uses extra memory for a copy, but keeps function safer to reuse.

nodes.title: Skip repeated fixed values for i
nodes.why: If the fixed number is the same as previous, it would generate duplicate triplets.
nodes.alternative: Collect all triplets then deduplicate at the end with a Set.
nodes.tradeoff: Slightly more loop logic, but avoids extra post-processing work.

nodes.title: Scan remaining range with two pointers
nodes.why: After fixing `i`, left/right pointers can find matching pairs in linear time.
nodes.alternative: Use a nested loop for all pairs after i.
nodes.tradeoff: Two pointers are faster, but require sorted order to be valid.

nodes.title: Move pointers based on sum sign
nodes.why: If sum is too small, move left up; if too large, move right down.
nodes.alternative: Recompute from scratch with hash lookups per pair.
nodes.tradeoff: Very efficient pointer movement, but tightly coupled to sorted array.

nodes.title: Record triplet, then move both sides
nodes.why: After finding a valid triplet, both pointers must move to search new combinations.
nodes.alternative: Move only one pointer and keep checking.
nodes.tradeoff: Cleaner progression with less repeated work after a match.

nodes.title: Skip duplicate values after each match
nodes.why: Prevents output like the same triplet repeated multiple times.
nodes.alternative: Allow duplicates and dedupe whole result later.
nodes.tradeoff: A couple of extra while checks, but output is unique by construction.
